***The Golden Compass*** is a 2007 [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy_film "Fantasy film") [adventure film](/wiki/Adventure_film "Adventure film") written and directed by [Chris Weitz](/wiki/Chris_Weitz "Chris Weitz"), based on the 1995 novel *[Northern Lights](/wiki/Northern_Lights_(Pullman_novel) "Northern Lights (Pullman novel)")* by [Philip Pullman](/wiki/Philip_Pullman "Philip Pullman"). It stars [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman"), [Sam Elliott](/wiki/Sam_Elliott "Sam Elliott"), [Eva Green](/wiki/Eva_Green "Eva Green"), [Dakota Blue Richards](/wiki/Dakota_Blue_Richards "Dakota Blue Richards") and [Daniel Craig](/wiki/Daniel_Craig "Daniel Craig"). In the film, Lyra joins a race of water-workers and seafarers on a trip to the far North in search of children kidnapped by the Gobblers, a group supported by the world's rulers, the Magisterium.

Development on the film was announced in February 2002, but difficulties over the screenplay and the selection of a director (including Weitz departing and returning) caused significant delays. Richards was cast as Lyra in June 2006, with Kidman and Craig joining soon thereafter. [Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") began that September at [Shepperton Studios](/wiki/Shepperton_Studios "Shepperton Studios") and lasted for several months. Location filming also took place in England, Switzerland, and Norway.

*The Golden Compass* premiered in [London](/wiki/London "London") on 27 November 2007, and was theatrically released in the United Kingdom by [Entertainment Film](/wiki/Entertainment_Film_Distributors "Entertainment Film Distributors") on 5 December and in the United States by [New Line Cinema](/wiki/New_Line_Cinema "New Line Cinema") on 7 December. The film received mixed reviews and grossed $372 million against a budget of $180 million. It won [Best Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects") at the [80th Academy Awards](/wiki/80th_Academy_Awards "80th Academy Awards") and [Best Special Visual Effects](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Special_Visual_Effects "BAFTA Award for Best Special Visual Effects") at the [61st British Academy Film Awards](/wiki/61st_British_Academy_Film_Awards "61st British Academy Film Awards").

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

On an alternate Earth, a church called the Magisterium controls people's beliefs under the auspices of [the Authority](/wiki/The_Authority_(His_Dark_Materials) "The Authority (His Dark Materials)"). Every person's soul exists as their own [animal companion](/wiki/Power_animal "Power animal"), a [dæmon](/wiki/D%C3%A6mon_(His_Dark_Materials) "Dæmon (His Dark Materials)"), and they must remain in close proximity. When young, people's dæmons change form reflecting their moods, but at the onset of puberty the dæmons settle into one form.

[Lyra Belacqua](/wiki/Lyra_Belacqua "Lyra Belacqua") is an orphan raised at [Jordan College](/wiki/Jordan_College,_Oxford "Jordan College, Oxford") with her dæmon [Pantalaimon](/wiki/Pantalaimon "Pantalaimon") or "Pan". Her uncle is [Lord Asriel](/wiki/Lord_Asriel "Lord Asriel"), an explorer and scholar of the college. When he returns from seeking the elusive [Dust](/wiki/Dust_(His_Dark_Materials) "Dust (His Dark Materials)") — cosmic particles that the Magisterium forbids being mentioned — Asriel reveals that Dust at the North Pole links to infinite worlds. He is granted funding for another expedition though, if proven, his theory would undermine the Magisterium's control.

Kidnappers called "the Gobblers" abduct children, including Lyra's friends, [Roger Parslow](/wiki/List_of_His_Dark_Materials_and_The_Book_of_Dust_characters "List of His Dark Materials and The Book of Dust characters") and Billy Costa, a young [Gyptian](/wiki/Races_and_creatures_in_His_Dark_Materials "Races and creatures in His Dark Materials").

[Marisa Coulter](/wiki/Marisa_Coulter "Marisa Coulter"), a wealthy "friend" of the college, invites Lyra to stay with her in [London](/wiki/London "London"). Just before she leaves, the [Master](/wiki/Master_(college) "Master (college)") of the college entrusts Lyra with her uncle's alethiometer, an artefact that reveals the truth, warning her to keep it secret. Mrs. Coulter becomes domineering after Lyra boasts of knowing about Dust. Lyra discovers she leads the Gobblers. After Mrs. Coulter's dæmon tries to steal the alethiometer, she and Pan flee, but are captured. A band of Gyptians save Lyra from the Gobblers, and she joins the Gyptian army sailing north to [Trollesund](/wiki/Locations_in_His_Dark_Materials "Locations in His Dark Materials") in [Norwegian Lapland](/wiki/Norway "Norway") to rescue their abducted children.

Gyptian elder [Farder Coram](/wiki/List_of_His_Dark_Materials_and_The_Book_of_Dust_characters "List of His Dark Materials and The Book of Dust characters") recognizes the alethiometer that, with his guidance, Lyra learns to decipher. [Serafina Pekkala](/wiki/Serafina_Pekkala "Serafina Pekkala"), queen of a witch clan, flies to the Gyptian ship and tells Lyra the children are at a Magisterial experimental station in [Bolvangar](/wiki/Locations_in_His_Dark_Materials "Locations in His Dark Materials"). In league with the Magisterium, Mrs. Coulter sends two venomous mechanical spy-flies after Lyra; one is caught by Farder Coram and sealed in a tin, but the other escapes.

Asriel reaches [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard"), the Kingdom of the Ice Bears, but is captured by [Samoyed](/wiki/Samoyedic_peoples "Samoyedic peoples") tribesmen hired by Mrs. Coulter, and imprisoned by the Ice Bears’ king, [Ragnar Sturlusson](/wiki/List_of_His_Dark_Materials_and_The_Book_of_Dust_characters "List of His Dark Materials and The Book of Dust characters"), who usurped the throne.

At Trollesund, Lyra befriends Texan [aeronaut](/wiki/Aeronaut "Aeronaut") Lee Scoresby, who suggests she hire him and his friend [Iorek Byrnison](/wiki/List_of_His_Dark_Materials_and_The_Book_of_Dust_characters "List of His Dark Materials and The Book of Dust characters"), an [armoured bear](/wiki/Races_and_creatures_in_His_Dark_Materials "Races and creatures in His Dark Materials"). Once a prince of the Ice Bears, Iorek was defeated in combat and exiled in shame. After Farder and Lyra find Iorek and help him recover his armour, he and Scoresby join the Gyptian trek to Bolvangar.

Following directions from the alethiometer, Lyra finds Billy Costa in a stupor clutching a dried fish in place of his dæmon. The Gobblers have experimented on him using "intercision", which separated him from his dæmon. They return to the Gyptian camp, which is attacked by Samoyeds who capture Lyra. She is taken to the Ice Bear king, Ragnar, with Iorek and Lee in pursuit in Lee's hot air balloon. Lyra tricks Ragnar into single combat with Iorek, who kills Ragnar and reclaims the throne.

Iorek carries Lyra to Bolvangar, but she is forced to cross a chasm over a narrow ice bridge alone before it collapses. Reaching the station, Lyra reunites with Roger. Caught by scientists, Lyra and Pan are thrown into the intercision chamber, but are rescued by Mrs. Coulter. She explains to Lyra that the Magisterium believe intercision protects children from Dust's corrupting influence, when their dæmon's form settles. Mrs. Coulter admits she could not let Lyra be intercised as she is her mother, who was forced to give her up; Lyra realises Asriel is her father. Mrs. Coulter asks for the alethiometer, but Lyra gives her the sealed tin containing the spy-fly, which stuns her mother. Lyra destroys the intercision machine, then leads the children outside, where they are confronted by [Tatar](/wiki/Tatars "Tatars") guards and their wolf dæmons. The guards are defeated by Iorek, Scoresby, the Gyptians, and witches led by Serafina.

Scoresby flies Lyra, Roger, Iorek, and Serafina north in his balloon in search of Asriel. Serafina relates a prophecy with Lyra at its crux in an upcoming war with the Magisterium, plotting to inflict their controlling Authority over all other worlds in the multiverse. Lyra commits to fighting the Magisterium.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Development")]

|  |
| --- |
| "Peter's operation was so impressive that, well, I realised the distance between me and Peter Jackson... At that moment, I realised the sheer scope of the endeavor. And I thought, 'You know what? I can't do this'." |
| — Director Chris Weitz on his initial departure from the project[[3]](#cite_note-newsweek-3) |

On 11 February 2002, following the success of New Line's *[The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings:_The_Fellowship_of_the_Ring "The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring")*, the studio bought the rights to [Philip Pullman](/wiki/Philip_Pullman "Philip Pullman")'s *[His Dark Materials](/wiki/His_Dark_Materials "His Dark Materials")* trilogy. In July 2003, [Tom Stoppard](/wiki/Tom_Stoppard "Tom Stoppard") was commissioned to write the screenplay.[[4]](#cite_note-news_etc-4) Directors [Brett Ratner](/wiki/Brett_Ratner "Brett Ratner") and [Sam Mendes](/wiki/Sam_Mendes "Sam Mendes") expressed interest in the film,[[4]](#cite_note-news_etc-4) but a year later, [Chris Weitz](/wiki/Chris_Weitz "Chris Weitz") was hired to direct after approaching the studio with an unsolicited 40-page treatment.[[11]](#cite_note-nyt-11) The studio rejected the script, asking Weitz to start from scratch. Since Weitz was an admirer of Stoppard's work, he decided not to read the adaptation in case he "subconsciously poached things from him."[[12]](#cite_note-empire2-12) After delivering his script, Weitz cited *[Barry Lyndon](/wiki/Barry_Lyndon "Barry Lyndon")* and *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* as stylistic influences on the film.[[4]](#cite_note-news_etc-4) In 2004, Weitz was invited by *[The Lord of the Rings](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings_(film_series) "The Lord of the Rings (film series)")* director [Peter Jackson](/wiki/Peter_Jackson "Peter Jackson") onto the set of *[King Kong](/wiki/King_Kong_(2005_film) "King Kong (2005 film)")* (2005) in order to gather information on directing a big-budget film, and to receive advice on dealing with New Line Cinema, for whom Jackson had worked on *Lord of the Rings*. After a subsequent interview in which Weitz said the novel's attacks on organised religion would have to be softened, he was criticised by some fans,[[3]](#cite_note-newsweek-3) and on 15 December 2004, Weitz resigned as director of the trilogy, citing the enormous technical challenges of the epic.[[4]](#cite_note-news_etc-4) He later indicated that he had envisioned the possibility of being denounced by both the book's fans and its detractors, as well as a studio hoping for another *Lord of the Rings*.[[3]](#cite_note-newsweek-3)

On 9 August 2005, British director [Anand Tucker](/wiki/Anand_Tucker "Anand Tucker") took over from Weitz. Tucker felt the film would thematically be about Lyra "looking for a family",[[4]](#cite_note-news_etc-4) and Pullman agreed: "He has plenty of very good ideas, and he isn't daunted by the technical challenges. But the best thing from the point of view of all who care about the story is his awareness that it isn't about computer graphics; it isn't about fantastic adventures in amazing-looking worlds; it's about Lyra."[[13]](#cite_note-ppo-13) Tucker resigned on 8 May 2006, citing creative disagreements with New Line, and Weitz returned to direct.[[4]](#cite_note-news_etc-4) Weitz said "I'm both the first and third director on the film ... but I did a lot of growing in the interim."[[14]](#cite_note-kidman-14)

According to producer [Deborah Forte](/wiki/Deborah_Forte "Deborah Forte"), Tucker wanted to make a smaller, less exciting film than New Line wanted. New Line production president [Toby Emmerich](/wiki/Toby_Emmerich "Toby Emmerich") said of Weitz's return: "I think Chris realised that if he didn't come back in and step up, maybe the movie wasn't going to get made ... We really didn't have a Plan B at that point."[[11]](#cite_note-nyt-11) Weitz was attracted back to the project after receiving a letter from Pullman asking him to reconsider. Since his departure, blueprints, production design and visual effects strategies had been put into position, and while Weitz admitted that his fears did not vanish, the project suddenly seemed feasible for the director.[[3]](#cite_note-newsweek-3)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Filming")]

Filming began at [Shepperton Studios](/wiki/Shepperton_Studios "Shepperton Studios") on 4 September 2006,[[4]](#cite_note-news_etc-4) with additional sequences shot in Switzerland and Norway.[[11]](#cite_note-nyt-11) Filming also took place at the [Old Royal Naval College](/wiki/Royal_Naval_College,_Greenwich "Royal Naval College, Greenwich") at [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich "Greenwich"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Chiswick House](/wiki/Chiswick_House "Chiswick House") in London, and in [Radcliffe Square](/wiki/Radcliffe_Square "Radcliffe Square"), [Christ Church, Oxford](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Oxford "Christ Church, Oxford"), [Exeter College, Oxford](/wiki/Exeter_College,_Oxford "Exeter College, Oxford"), [The Queen's College, Oxford](/wiki/The_Queen%27s_College,_Oxford "The Queen's College, Oxford"), [The Historic Dockyard Chatham](/wiki/Chatham_Dockyard "Chatham Dockyard")[[16]](#cite_note-16) and [Hedsor House](/wiki/Hedsor_House "Hedsor House") in Buckinghamshire.

### Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Design")]

Production designer [Dennis Gassner](/wiki/Dennis_Gassner "Dennis Gassner") says of his work on the film:

> The whole project is about translation—translation from something you would understand into something that is in a different vernacular. So, it's a new signature, looking into another world that seems familiar but is still unique. There's a term I use—called 'cludging'—it's taking one element and combining it with another element to make something new. It's a hybrid or amalgamation, and that's what this movie is about from a design perspective. It's about amalgamating ideas and concepts and theoretical and physical environments.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[Rhythm & Hues Studios](/wiki/Rhythm_%26_Hues_Studios "Rhythm & Hues Studios") (R&H) created the main dæmons and [Framestore CFC](/wiki/Framestore_CFC "Framestore CFC") created all the bears.[[19]](#cite_note-19) British company [Cinesite](/wiki/Cinesite "Cinesite") created the secondary dæmons.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Differences from the novel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Differences from the novel")]

Numerous scenes from the novel did not feature in the film or were markedly changed. On 7 December 2007, *[New York](/wiki/New_York_(magazine) "New York (magazine)")* magazine reviewed draft scripts from both Stoppard and Weitz; both were significantly longer than the final version, and Weitz's draft (which, unlike Stoppard's, did not feature significant additions to the source material) was pronounced the best of the three. The magazine concluded that instead of a "likely three hours of running time" that included such scenes as Mrs. Coulter's London party and Lyra's meeting with a witch representative, the studio had opted for a "failed"[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] length of under 2 hours in order to maximize revenue.[[21]](#cite_note-Vulture-21)

On 9 October 2007, Weitz revealed that the final 3 chapters from *Northern Lights* had been moved to the film's potential sequel, *The Subtle Knife*, in order to provide "the most promising conclusion to the first film and the best possible beginning to the second,"[[22]](#cite_note-hdmorg-weitz-22) though he also said less than a month later that there had been "tremendous marketing pressure" to create "an upbeat ending."[[23]](#cite_note-NYT-23) (The *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")* found this "truncated" ending abrupt.[[24]](#cite_note-24)) Author Pullman publicly supported these changes, saying that "every film has to make changes to the story that the original book tells—not to change the outcome, but to make it fit the dimensions and the medium of film."[[25]](#cite_note-hdmorg-pullman-25) In addition to removing the novel's unsettling ending, the film reverses the order in which Lyra travels to Bolvangar, the Gobbler's outpost, and then [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard"), the armoured bears' kingdom.[[26]](#cite_note-AVC-26) (Neither deviation from the book features in [Scholastic Publishing](/wiki/Scholastic_Publishing "Scholastic Publishing")'s *The Golden Compass: The Story of the Movie* novelisation.) In July 2009, Weitz told a [Comic Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con_International "San Diego Comic-Con International") audience that the film had been "recut by [New Line], and my experience with it ended being quite a terrible one";[[27]](#cite_note-CinemaBlend-27) he also told *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine that he had felt that by "being faithful to the book I was working at odds with the studio."[[28]](#cite_note-time.com-28)

Tasha Robinson of *[The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club")* argued that through the use of a spoken introduction and other exposition-filled dialogue, the film fails by "baldly revealing up front everything that the novel is trying to get you to wonder about and to explore slowly."[[26]](#cite_note-AVC-26) Youyoung Lee wrote in a December 2007 *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* that the film "leaves out the gore", such as the book's ritualistic heart-eating that concludes the bear fight, "to create family-friendlier fare."[[29]](#cite_note-29) Lee also said that the film "downplays the Magisterium's religious nature", but Robinson argued that the depiction of the church in the film is as "a hierarchical organisation of formally robed, iconography-heavy priests who dictate and define morality for their followers, are based out of cathedrals, and decry teachings counter to theirs as 'heresy.' ... doing ugly things to children under cover of secrecy." Robinson then asks, "Who are most people going to think of besides the Catholic Church?"[[26]](#cite_note-AVC-26)

Although the character of Mrs. Coulter has black hair in the novel, Pullman responded to the blonde Kidman's portrayal by saying, "I was clearly wrong. You sometimes are wrong about your characters. She's blonde. She has to be."[[30]](#cite_note-butler-30)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Controversies")]

Prior to release, the film faced criticism from [secularist](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism") and religious organisations due to the source material's anti-religious themes.[[27]](#cite_note-CinemaBlend-27) Several key themes of the novels, such as the rejection of religion and the [abuse of power](/wiki/Abuse_of_power "Abuse of power") in a fictionalized version of the [Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church"), were diluted in the adaptation. Director Weitz said that "in the books the Magisterium is a version of the Catholic Church gone wildly astray from its roots", but that the organization portrayed in his film would not directly match that of Pullman's books. Instead, the Magisterium represents all [dogmatic](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma") organizations.[[31]](#cite_note-telegraph-31)

Attempting to reassure fans of the novels, Weitz said that religion would instead appear in euphemistic terms, yet the decision was criticized by some fans,[[32]](#cite_note-btts-weitz-32) anti-censorship groups, and the [National Secular Society](/wiki/National_Secular_Society "National Secular Society") (of which Pullman is an honorary associate), which said "they are taking the heart out of it, losing the point of it, castrating it ..."[[33]](#cite_note-observer-33) and "this is part of a long-term problem over freedom of speech." *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")* wrote: "With $180 million at stake, the studio opted to kidnap the book's body and leave behind its soul."[[34]](#cite_note-atlantic-34) The changes from the novel have been present since Tom Stoppard's rejected version of the script,[[11]](#cite_note-nyt-11) and Pullman expected the film to be "faithful",[[31]](#cite_note-telegraph-31) although he also said: "They do know where to put the theology and that's off the film."[[34]](#cite_note-atlantic-34) A *[Christianity Today](/wiki/Christianity_Today "Christianity Today")* review of the film noted that "'[Magisterium](/wiki/Magisterium "Magisterium")' does refer, in the real world, to the teaching authority of the Catholic Church, and the film [is] peppered with religiously significant words like 'oblation' and '[heresy](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy")'", adding that when one character smashes through the wall of a Magisterium building, the damaged exterior is "decorated with [Christian] [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") icons."[[35]](#cite_note-CT-35)

On 7 October 2007, the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(US) "Catholic League (US)") called for a boycott of the film.[[36]](#cite_note-CL-36) League president [William A. Donohue](/wiki/William_A._Donohue "William A. Donohue") said he would not ordinarily object to the film, but that while the religious elements are diluted from the source material, the film will encourage children to read the novels, which he says denigrate Christianity and promote atheism for children.[[37]](#cite_note-fox-37) He cited Pullman telling *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* in 2001 that he is trying to undermine the basis of Christian belief.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The league hoped that "the film [would fail] to meet box-office expectations and that [Pullman's] books attract few buyers",[[39]](#cite_note-39) declaring the boycott campaign a success after a North American opening weekend which was lower than anticipated.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[Albert Mohler](/wiki/Albert_Mohler "Albert Mohler"), the president of the [Southern Baptist Theological Seminary](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Theological_Seminary "Southern Baptist Theological Seminary"), agreed that the broad appeal of the film was a dangerous lure to the novels, which he criticised for carrying a clear agenda to expose what Pullman believes is the "tyranny of the Christian faith" and for providing "a liberating mythology for a new secular age."[[41]](#cite_note-41) Denny Wayman of the [Free Methodist Church](/wiki/Free_Methodist_Church "Free Methodist Church") made the assertion that *The Golden Compass* is a "film trying to preach an atheistic message."[[42]](#cite_note-Cinema_in_Focus-Golden_Compass_Anti-Christian-42) Other evangelical groups, such as the [Christian Film and Television Commission](/wiki/Ted_Baehr "Ted Baehr"), adopted a "wait-and-see" approach to the film before deciding upon any action,[[43]](#cite_note-43) as did the [Catholic Church in England and Wales](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_England_and_Wales "Catholic Church in England and Wales").[[44]](#cite_note-nitwits-44) Theologian Donna Freitas argued that the books were "deeply theological, and deeply Christian in their theology".[[45]](#cite_note-45) In November 2007, a review of the film by the director and staff reviewer of the Office for Film and Broadcasting of the [United States Conference of Catholic Bishops](/wiki/United_States_Conference_of_Catholic_Bishops "United States Conference of Catholic Bishops") (USCCB) appeared on the website of the Catholic News Service and in Catholic newspapers across the country. The review suggested that instead of a boycott, it may be appropriate for Catholic parents to "talk through any thorny philosophical issues" with their children.[[46]](#cite_note-46) However, on 10 December 2007 the review was removed from the website at the USCCB's request.[[47]](#cite_note-47) On 19 December 2007, the [Vatican](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") newspaper, *[L'Osservatore Romano](/wiki/L%27Osservatore_Romano "L'Osservatore Romano")*, published an editorial in which it denounced the film as godless.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Pullman said of Donohue's call for a boycott, "Why don't we trust readers? Why don't we trust filmgoers? Oh, it causes me to shake my head with sorrow that such nitwits could be loose in the world."[[44]](#cite_note-nitwits-44) In a discussion with Donohue on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")'s *[Early Show](/wiki/Early_Show "Early Show")*, [Ellen Johnson](/wiki/Ellen_Johnson "Ellen Johnson"), president of [American Atheists](/wiki/American_Atheists "American Atheists"), said that rather than promote atheism, the film would encourage children to question authority, saying that would not be a bad thing for children to learn.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Director Weitz says that he believes *His Dark Materials* is "not an atheistic work, but a highly spiritual and reverent piece of writing",[[32]](#cite_note-btts-weitz-32) and [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman") defended her decision to star in the film, saying that the source material had been "watered down a little" and that her religious beliefs would prevent her from taking a role in a film she perceived as anti-Catholic.[[14]](#cite_note-kidman-14) Some commentators indicated that they believed both sides' criticism would prove ultimately impotent and that the negative publicity would prove a boon for the film's box-office.[[44]](#cite_note-nitwits-44)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) Sales were in fact poor; one week after the film's release, [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") said of the Catholic campaign, "any bad buzz on a family film can be mortal, and that seems to have been the case this time."[[52]](#cite_note-52) The planned film trilogy has not been continued, prompting actor [Sam Elliott](/wiki/Sam_Elliott "Sam Elliott") to blame censorship and the Catholic Church.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Release")]

### Theatrical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Theatrical")]

The film premiered in [London](/wiki/London "London") on 27 November 2007, and was released on 5 December 2007, in British cinemas by [Entertainment Film Distributors](/wiki/Entertainment_Film_Distributors "Entertainment Film Distributors") and released on 7 December 2007, in American theaters by New Line Cinema.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Home media")]

The film was released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") on 29 April 2008.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Shortly before the film's release, Weitz suggested that an extended cut of the film could be released on DVD, saying "I'd really love to do a fuller cut of the film"; he further speculated that such a version "could probably end up at two-and-a-half hours."[[55]](#cite_note-55) This proposed cut would presumably not include the original ending: MTV reported in December 2007 that Weitz hoped to include that material at the beginning of a possible *[The Subtle Knife](/wiki/The_Subtle_Knife "The Subtle Knife")* adaptation, and that a *Compass* Director's Cut might feature "a moment" of it as a "teaser."[[56]](#cite_note-MTV-56) so far, however, no extended version has been released, as of 2014.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

On 9 June 2020 Weitz revealed on Twitter that it would take $17 million for him to complete VFX for his directors cut making him think there is no financial incentive for them to finish it and release it.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box-office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Box-office")]

The North American opening weekend return was "a little disappointing" for New Line Cinema,[[59]](#cite_note-59) earning US$25.8 million with total domestic box-office of $70 million compared to an estimated $180 million production budget.[[1]](#cite_note-mojo-1) Despite this, the film's fortunes rebounded as its performance outside the United States was described as "stellar" by *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")*,[[60]](#cite_note-60) and as "astonishing" by New Line.[[61]](#cite_note-61) In the United Kingdom, the film grossed $53,198,635 and became the second-highest-grossing non-sequel of 2007 there (behind *[The Simpsons Movie](/wiki/The_Simpsons_Movie "The Simpsons Movie")*). In Japan, the film was officially released in March 2008 on 700 screens, ultimately grossing $33,501,399; but previews of the film between 23 and 24 February 2008, earned $2.5 million. By 6 July 2008, it had earned $302,127,136 internationally, totaling $372,234,864 worldwide.[[1]](#cite_note-mojo-1) Overseas rights to the film were sold to fund the $180 million production budget for the film, so most of these profits did not go to New Line.[[62]](#cite_note-62) This has been cited as a possible "last straw" in [Time Warner](/wiki/Time_Warner "Time Warner")'s decision to merge New Line Cinema into [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures").[[63]](#cite_note-Guardian-63)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Critical response")]

Reviews of *The Golden Compass* were mixed.[[64]](#cite_note-latimes-64) On the review aggregator website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film has an approval rating of 42%, based on 196 reviews, with an [average score](/wiki/Weighted_arithmetic_mean "Weighted arithmetic mean") of 5.60/10. The critical consensus reads: "Without the bite or the controversy of the source material, *The Golden Compass* is reduced to impressive visuals overcompensating for lax storytelling."[[65]](#cite_note-65) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the film has a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_mean "Weighted mean") score of 51 out of 100, based on reviews from 33 critics, indicating "mixed or average reviews".[[66]](#cite_note-66) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "B" on an A+ to F scale.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

[Manohla Dargis](/wiki/Manohla_Dargis "Manohla Dargis") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* said that the film "crams so many events, characters, ... twists and turns, sumptuously appointed rooms and ethereally strange vistas ... that [it] risks losing you in the whirl" and that while *The Golden Compass* is "an honorable work," it is "hampered by its fealty to the book and its madly rushed pace."[[68]](#cite_note-68) James Berardinelli of *ReelReviews* gave the film 2.5 stars out of 4, calling it "adequate, but not inspired" and criticising the first hour for its rushed pace and sketchily-developed characters.[[69]](#cite_note-69) James Christopher of *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* of London was disappointed, praising the "marvellous" special effects and casting, but saying that the "books weave a magic the film simply cannot match" and citing a "lack of genuine drama."[[70]](#cite_note-70)

*[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* rated it an "A−" and called it a "good, if familiar fantasy," saying "The find is Dakota Blue Richards ... who's both grounded and magical."[[71]](#cite_note-Corliss-71) [Peter Bradshaw](/wiki/Peter_Bradshaw "Peter Bradshaw") of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* rated it 4 stars out of 5, praising Nicole Kidman's casting and saying it had "no other challengers as [2007's] big Christmas movie."[[72]](#cite_note-72) [Leonard Maltin](/wiki/Leonard_Maltin "Leonard Maltin") gave the film 3 out of 4 stars, and said that "Richards is persuasive" and that it "does a good job of introducing us to an unfamiliar world." Critic [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") awarded the film 4 out of 4 stars and called it "a darker, deeper fantasy epic than the 'Rings' trilogy, '[The Chronicles of Narnia](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia_(film_series) "The Chronicles of Narnia (film series)")' or the '[Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series) "Harry Potter (film series)")' films," saying that it "creates villains that are more complex and poses more intriguing questions. As a visual experience, it is superb. As an escapist fantasy, it is challenging ... I think [it] is a wonderfully good-looking movie, with exciting passages and a captivating heroine."[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Pullman was described by an interviewer from *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* in Britain as sounding "ambivalent" and "guarded" about the film, saying in March 2008: "A lot of things about it were good... Nothing's perfect. Nothing can bring out all that's in the book. There are always compromises." He hoped, however, that the rest of the trilogy would be adapted with the same cast and crew.[[74]](#cite_note-74) In July 2009, after this possibility had been exhausted, Weitz told *Time* magazine that he thought the film's special effects ended up being its "most successful element."[[28]](#cite_note-time.com-28)

Debbie Day of *[Premiere](/wiki/Premiere_(magazine) "Premiere (magazine)")* magazine said "*The Golden Compass* ultimately fails as a film in its broad strokes and inadequate scene development."[[75]](#cite_note-75)

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Music")]

Further information: [The Golden Compass (soundtrack)](/wiki/The_Golden_Compass_(soundtrack) "The Golden Compass (soundtrack)")

French composer [Alexandre Desplat](/wiki/Alexandre_Desplat "Alexandre Desplat") composed the film's music. British singer [Kate Bush](/wiki/Kate_Bush "Kate Bush") wrote and performed the song "[Lyra](/wiki/Lyra_(song) "Lyra (song)")" which is played over the end credits.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The film's soundtrack album was released on 11 December 2007, by [New Line Records](/wiki/WaterTower_Music "WaterTower Music").

## Video game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Video game")]

Main article: [The Golden Compass (video game)](/wiki/The_Golden_Compass_(video_game) "The Golden Compass (video game)")

The video game for this film was released in November 2007 in Europe and December 2007 in North America and Australia for the [PC](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer"), [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii"), [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2"), [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3"), [PlayStation Portable](/wiki/PlayStation_Portable "PlayStation Portable"), [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS") and the [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360"). It was developed by [Shiny Entertainment](/wiki/Shiny_Entertainment "Shiny Entertainment") and published by [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega").[[80]](#cite_note-80)

Players take control of the characters Lyra Belacqua and Iorek Byrnison in Lyra's attempt to save her friend Roger from the General Oblation Board. As this game does not fully take into account the changes made by the final version of the film, a small amount of footage from the film's deleted ending can be viewed near the end of the game, and the order in which Lyra travels to Bolvangar and Svalbard follows the book and not the film.

## Future

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Future")]

### Cancelled sequels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Cancelled sequels")]

At the time of *The Golden Compass*'s theatrical release, Weitz pledged to "protect [the] integrity" of the prospective sequels by being "much less compromising" in the book-to-film adaptation process.[[23]](#cite_note-NYT-23) New Line Cinema commissioned [Hossein Amini](/wiki/Hossein_Amini "Hossein Amini") to write a screenplay based on the second book in the trilogy, *[The Subtle Knife](/wiki/The_Subtle_Knife "The Subtle Knife")*, potentially for release in May 2010, with the third book of the trilogy, *[The Amber Spyglass](/wiki/The_Amber_Spyglass "The Amber Spyglass")*, to follow. However, New Line president Toby Emmerich stressed that production of the second and third films was dependent on the financial success of *The Golden Compass*.[[81]](#cite_note-81) When *The Golden Compass* did not meet expectations at the United States box-office, the likelihood of a sequel was downplayed by New Line. According to studio co-head [Michael Lynne](/wiki/Michael_Lynne "Michael Lynne"), "The jury is still very much out on the movie, and while it's performed very strongly overseas, we'll look at it early 2008 and see where we're going with a sequel."[[82]](#cite_note-82)

In February 2008, Weitz told *[The Daily Yomiuri](/wiki/The_Daily_Yomiuri "The Daily Yomiuri")*, a Japanese newspaper, that he still hoped for the sequels' production: "at first it looked like we were down for the count because in the U.S. [the film] underperformed, but then internationally it performed [better] than expectations. So, a lot depends on Japan, frankly... I think if it does well enough here we'll be in good shape for that."[[83]](#cite_note-YM-83) Although producer Deborah Forte had, in March 2008, expressed optimism that the sequels would be made,[[84]](#cite_note-84) by October 2008, the two planned sequels were officially placed on hold, according to New Line Cinema, because of financial concerns during the global recession.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86) Sam Elliott, however, stated, "The Catholic Church ... lambasted them, and I think it scared New Line off."[[87]](#cite_note-87)

### Television reboot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Golden_Compass_(film)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Television reboot")]

From 2019, 12 years after the film's disappointment that caused the two sequels to be scrapped, a three-season television series adaptation of all three novels of *[His Dark Materials](/wiki/His_Dark_Materials_(TV_series) "His Dark Materials (TV series)")* was made, culminating in 2022. It starred [Dafne Keen](/wiki/Dafne_Keen "Dafne Keen") as Lyra and [Ruth Wilson](/wiki/Ruth_Wilson "Ruth Wilson") as Mrs Coulter. It was produced by [Bad Wolf](/wiki/Bad_Wolf_(production_company) "Bad Wolf (production company)") and [New Line Production](/wiki/New_Line_Cinema "New Line Cinema") and was broadcast on both [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One") and [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO"). The series followed the novels more closely, retaining more nuances of the story-line, and received a much better reception than the film adaptation.[[88]](#cite_note-88)
